sentences.text: I have to laugh because when-- when my uncle told me after the war what was in this carriage, that was a great source of pride to me because I was running around telling my friends that I was a partisan.
Even though I didn't know anything what was in this carriage, you know.
But hey, I contributed in some way to the war effort.
So that was-- that was very important.
It was important that as many people know as possible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another incident that I remember was, one winter when most of the people in our hut were sick with typhus.
I don't know if I had it too, or I didn't have it, or if I had it, I had it in a milder form.
I didn't have such a high fever.
So I went out to chop some wood, which, again, some of Babushka's friends had brought to the hut, but it was lying outside in the snow.
And also outside there, there was this big-- to me it looked like a giant, rusty ax.
And I could hardly pick it up and I needed to chop this wood to bring it to warm up in the hut, to make people feel a little better.
Mind you, most of them were delirious and they didn't know what's going on with themselves anyway.
But I was trying to chop this wood because this was the thing to do, because I wanted to help.
And of course I-- I couldn't.
And I-- the ax fell on my finger.
And my finger started bleeding.
And I started-- I just stood there frozen and I started to cry.
And out of nowhere, this Romanian soldier appeared.
And I was terrified, because wherever they were soldiers, there was death.
But he-- he didn't approach me right away.
He sort of gave me some time.
And then he looked at me, and he had good in his face, like it wasn't bad.
And he-- I let him come closer, and he didn't say a word.
He just came closer, and he pushed me away a little bit.
And he took the ax and he started to chop the wood.
And then he looked into the hut and he saw what's-- what's happening there.
His name was Peter.
Well, in Romanian, his name was Petre.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he started to come to the hut [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and bring medication, and bring food, from time to time.
And this might have well saved a few lives, which might not have made it without medication or without this, particularly the food.
And then, one day, when some of the people in the hut started to-- were able to start to talk, he told us that he was married to a Jewish girl and he had a four-year-old son.
And after he had been taken to the army, they were shipped up-- shipped out to some camp and he-- he never found out where they were.
And-- or if they're still alive, for that matter.
And after about two weeks, Petre disappeared and we never saw him again.
And we never knew if he was caught helping us and was shot or punished in some way, or if his unit simply moved on.
But it was nice that he was there for as long as he was there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I must say, in retrospect, that he took a risk helping us.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know, because I never felt safe, whether I was with them or not with them.
My only concern was that Tosia should like me.
So when my fingers were bleeding and my hands were bleeding from the knitting, that was a source of pain for me too.
Because maybe now I don't have the hair and now I have my bleeding hands.
And that-- so that was one preoccupation that was almost permanent.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I don't know.
It-- I just-- I think I just took it for granted that that's the way it is.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes what helped me was that I would sort of stare into nothing, and mentally go home, and remember the games we played in the attic, and the clothes that my friends took out from old trunks that my people had put away and-- and the-- the-- the dressing up.
And we were playing that we were such and such's wife, and the-- the ranks were the king-- so-- so the king's wife.
So it was the king's wife and it was Antonescu's wife and it was Hitler's wife and it was God's wife.
So Hitler was right beside God.
Right?
But Hitler's wife could-- had more power than anybody else's wife.
So if you played that role, you've had it for the day.
I mean, you were somebody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used to go back home and sort of be in the store with my dad, mentally, or go to my grandmother's store, or go walking in the park.
There was a lot of day dreaming and a lot of-- there was-- there were no books.
There was nothing that I could do.

So I think the only way I could use my mind-- or maybe these were like stepping stones to sanity, to-- to-- my way of running away temporarily from the reality that I was in and going-- going back home where I find myself in shul and we're running around and the men are shouting, quiet, quiet, it's davening, you know.
And then all these things used to come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then-- well, that's how we lived for-- for three and 1/2 years.
And then the war turned, and then we knew that the withdrawing SS troops slaughter everybody they find alive.
So people started to dig holes under the huts and cover them with camouflaged boards or with what have you.
But these were literally holes.
I mean, it wasn't like a cellar or something.
But it was a hole in the ground where we were planning to hide when-- when the Germans withdraw.
And we knew when they withdrew from [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know if everybody else knew.
So we-- we were in the hut there, in the-- in the hole.
And I mean, even getting down there, was-- it was such a narrow hole to get down there that you could hardly sort of slide through it.
And-- and down there it was dark and it was wet.
And then the baby was crying and people were moaning.
And it was like living in a hole in the ground.
I-- I'm not even sure that it was-- if it was a few days or if it was a few hours.
I-- I don't remember.
But I remember that, suddenly, we heard Russian songs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father said we must not get out because this could be a ploy from the SS to lure people out and kill them, slaughter them.
So he said no, we're going to be smarter.
We're going to just stay here.
And we did, until my Uncle Armin came from the partisans and said it's OK.

And the people who have liberated us, before the regular Russian army came in, which was a few hours later, were boys between 14 and 17 who had been too young to be taken to the Army at the beginning of the war and who formed these partisan groups in the forest.
And they came in, and they-- they got the-- they were on-- on the heels of the withdrawing armies, and they saved a lot of lives.
Among them were some Jewish boys who-- who joined them.
A lot of them were Ukrainians.
And while they were very antisemitic, a lot of them, here, they felt that they are reconquering their fatherland, their own territory.
So they were really into it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that happened in the spring of 1944.
And immediately, of course, people started to make plans to go back home.
But back home, Romania was still occupied by the Germans, by the Nazis.
So some people started walking.
There were no means of transportation.
All the trains, all the trucks, everything was mobilized for the war.
So there was no way you could find some wheels to roll on.
So people started walking by foot.
It was not a terrible distance.
It was maybe 200, 300 kilometers-- or miles, I'm not sure.
So a lot of people started to walk.
Other people were too weak and too sick, so they stayed put in these huts, hoping that they'll gain some strength now that there was more food available.
And I believe we stayed for a week or two, and then we-- we started on our way too.
And then on the way, Uncle Armin had some-- and-- and the babushka gave us some bottles of homemade vodka, which they gave us not for drinking, but they give it to us for bribing.
Because if a truck of Russian soldiers would drive by and you'd give them a bottle of vodka, they'd let you ride with them for a few hours, or a few minutes, or what have you.
So that's what that was used for.
So part of the way we had some rides with Russian soldiers, or with peasants with a carriage, with a horse.
Part of the way we walked.
But wherever we were, it was immediately behind the front lines.
In some cities when we walked in, there was still street fighting, and there was bombing, and the water was poisoned.
And at the same-- other times, we were walking on these fields and we had no food, and Russian planes would throw down canned food.
And it-- it would be a massive amount of the same kind of thing.
Like it would be-- for awhile it was these thick, very thick bars of chocolate, like army chocolate.
And we would have that, and only that.
For a while it was cans of caviar.
And to this day, I cannot stand the smell of caviar, because caviar is delicious if you eat it with something.
But try and eat just caviar, it's not so delicious.
But hey, if there's nothing else, you eat it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's how we arrived to Czernowitz, which used to be the capital of Bukovina.
And from then, we were only two hours away by train, approximately two hours away by train.
But again, there were no trains.
And Czernowitz had just been occupied when we arrived.
And we had had some relatives there who lived there before.
We tried to find them.
I don't believe we did.

But what happened a few days after we were there was that the Russians started capturing men on the street, regardless of who they were, and dragged them deep into Russia to have them work in the cold mine-- in the coal mines to help the war effort.
And they were snatching anybody who was on the street, shoving them into trucks, not even letting them tell their families what happened to them, and-- and take them away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, when we heard that, we decide-- the men were the decision makers, of course, at that time-- they decided to rent a truck.
And we escaped to Kishinev, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which had been occupied from earlier times.
So this capturing of man or people on the street had already finished by that time.
So we lived in a room.
There was this woman who rented us this room, but the room didn't have a door to the outside.
And she would not allow us to go through her room.
So the only way to get into our room was through the window.
So we were going in and out through the window.
I think we were there for a few months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember anything about what I did there during the day.
And then we started walking again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Actually, I believe I went to school, but I don't have really any clear memories of that.
And then we start walking again.
And we walked towards home again.
Everybody was walking towards home, primarily because people were interested in who-- in survivors, because that was the only place where you know you could meet people who remained alive.
By the way, my paternal grandparents both died before the war ended.
My maternal grandmother survived, but she got gangrene on one of her fingers.
And it was a-- an unbelievable sight to see how this finger is withering away on a live body, and eventually just fell off.
How and why it didn't attack the rest of her hand, I'll never know, but it didn't.
And she survived.
Of course when we got home, we were all like skeletons.
And because our house was one of the better ones in town, with flowing water and all this, when we got there, we found the firemen had occupied the house.
But they told us that before that, there was a German hospital in the house, and there was a-- later was a Russian hospital in the house.
And then the firemen came.
So obviously there wasn't a stitch of anything left from our belongings, money.
And I don't believe that in any other house there was either, because the-- the local population would just come in and plunder everything out.
However, before we left, my father boarded up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some corners in the attic and stuffed in a lot of things there.
Among them were needles from his store, sugar, soap, toothpaste, all kinds of these things.
The jewelery he buried under a tree in the backyard.
So that was impossible to retrieve, because that was public and you don't-- you don't risk that.
However, the stuff in the attic remained intact.
It didn't dawn on anybody that there's something in there.

And it was a lifesaver, because there weren't any needles after the war, because all the metal-- I think-- I don't know what metal exactly needles are made-- made from, but whatever they're made from was not available because of the war.
And people-- I mean, you could get food for three days for a bloody needle, which was-- it was fantastic.
The flour, the soap, the toothpaste, everything was intact, just the way he left it.
But the needles were the real treasure.
That was-- that was very, very important.
And then, of course, Romania turned communist.
And after having gone through the camps, the theory of communism was very appealing to me as a teenager.
My mother sort of had a leftist orientation from the Zionist organization, so now she became even more.
And the-- the thing was that the only way this is never going to happen again is if we all become communist.
And then we're all brothers and then we're all equal and all the bullshit which never happened.
And I became very actively involved in the communist youth organization.
And I had a lot of organizing skill since I was small.
And I honed them, and I became a member of the Central Committee, and I was traveling to Bucharest.
And I was-- I mean, I was a kid, but I was doing things which were pretty adult.
And-- and I remained very hooked on communism until I finished university, this-- because the school system also brainwashes you every minute.
And only until I finished university and started looking around at the reality of life did I realize that communism ain't what it-- what it says in books it is.
And then I had a tremendous depression, tremendous depression, because I had invested myself completely, emotionally, into this idea.
And the fact that I suddenly realized that it's not what I thought it was was a terrible blow-- terrible blow.
Before that, mind you, my parents wanted to leave.
My-- my parents woke up earlier.
My-- my father was never a communist.
My mother woke up before me.
And they wanted to leave to Israel.
But there was no way I would leave.

And I was the only child, so of course they didn't leave, which is a great source of sadness for me, because had they left to Israel in the late '40s rather than at the early '60s, they might have still made a life for themselves in Israel and learned the language and-- and adapted.
And in the late '60s they were both old and-- and they just couldn't make it there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, my Uncle Armin and Aunt Mila had ran away to Belgium, where they lived for several years, where their two children were born, Diana and Murray.
And from there, they immigrated to Canada.
And they brought us out from Israel to Canada.
They used to live in Montreal.
We were for two years in Israel, from '61 to '63.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had all kinds of interesting jobs in Israel, and-- and really, Israel, for me, was the best thing.
I mean, I loved it, and I loved life there.
And I was always active and I always liked to live in the life of the-- in eye of the tornado, not on the periphery.
And Israel certainly gives you that.
Every day of your life, you're in-- in the eye of the tornado.
And I loved it.
But my parents couldn't handle it.
So we decided to-- to leave and to go to Montreal.
Before-- when we came back to Romania, my father gave a Romanian soldier some gold coins and stuff, and he brought Auntie Sidi back from the depth of Russia to Romania.
Then she left to Israel a lot earlier than we did.
My grandmother died in 1953, in Romania.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Auntie Sidi was now in Israel, where she still is.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Uncle Armin and Auntie Mila and their kids lived in Montreal, and they took us out there too.
So that's how I got to Canada.
I immediately started to study.
I'd lost a lot of schooling and stuff, so I immediately-- actually, I had a master's degree from Romania.
Because when I finished high school, Russian became the second language in Romania, instead of-- replacing French, which it used to be before.
And they didn't have any Russian teachers.
So they needed a large number of Russian teachers to supply to all the schools.

So in order to ensure that this is going to be a successful program, they went through all the schools and picked out the students who finished their matriculation with the highest grades, and put them in this institute of Russian language and literature in Bucharest, which was an intensive immersion program in Russian language and literature.
And I did so well.
I wanted to study medicine, but I had no choice.
Like in Romania, you do what the five-year economic plan dictates, and that's what it dictated then.
So I did so well that I remained as a teacher at-- at this institute.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then I-- I worked at a newspaper in Romania and this teaching position.
And I was very involved with academic life and with academia and with theater and with opera, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I got that depression and I decided to leave with my parents.
And then I was fired from everywhere.
And also, applying to leave Romania was a very risky thing, because there was no rhyme-- we thought there was no rhyme or reason to who gets the visas-- the exit visas-- and who doesn't.
I later found out that there was some rhyme and reason.
But from the outside, it appeared like there wasn't.
So when you applied, you didn't know if it's going to last a year, or if you're going to rot there for 20 years, or if you're going to rot there forever as an enemy of the country.
So eventually, I-- after I applied, I got some--
I couldn't find the job, because in your identity card, line number four said what your profession was.

And if they look at your profession and you are an assistant at the university, and you apply for a job as a cashier in a store or-- or in a bus, they know that there's some political issue involved here, so they're not hiring you.
So eventually I ended up working at the garbage-- at cleaning streets.
I was sweeping streets, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was-- actually, turned out to be not too bad, once I got used to sweeping the streets, because I was very alert.
And I soon found out who lives where and who has an affair with whom and who brings flowers to whom and whatever, like the whole thing.
I-- I was sweeping, but I was watching.
And it turned out to be interesting.
Until one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the government built an incin-- was-- they were planning to build an incinerator in Bucharest.

And they were selecting samples of garbage from different streets and different areas to put them through the lab to see how much energy-- agricultural, domestic, or industrial garbage, and I can tell you all the details about that-- would generate for using-- for how much energy it would generate to be used for warming-- for heating a student dorms or whatever other public facilities.
And somehow, the streets- the one street in my area which I was sweeping was chosen for that.

And I was asked to select samples every day, and I was given this little glass tube, and I was supposed to mark on it at what time I selected it and where, and is it domestic garbage or is it leaves or what have you.
And, I mean, I-- I did what I was supposed to do.
But I had a good handwriting.
I mean, I had finished high school.
I was well read.
I had finished university.
So my samples were-- looked a lot better than many of the other samples, which were taken by people who are not as literate or as educated.

And that got me into a whole new, very interesting and sad story, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] namely, the person who was in charge with the lab, with all the research for this incinerator, was a Jewish woman who had never been deported.
She lived in Israel.
And one day she decided to come and see who is sending these samples so nicely written.
And we got acquainted, and we befriended one another.
She was a lot older than I was, but we became friends.

And she decided that she's going to take me to the lab to help her, because I knew Russian and German, and a lot of the equipment that was brought in for this incinerator, was in Ru-- the prospect for them was in Russian or in German, so-- neither of which she knew, so I could help her with that.
Eventually she also taught me how to do some chemical testing of stuff, like put it in-- I don't know what you call that, where you dry things out fast.
Some kind of a chemical thing.
To-- to decide what minerals it contains and what have you.
In the meantime, she had a very elderly mother who was very sick.
And often she would leave the lab before the end of the day, or she would come late in the morning.
And I had the keys and I knew what to do.
And she'd go to see her mother in the morning or she'd go to see her at her lunchtime or in-- after or before the day ended.
And sometimes she wouldn't come back.
And I would take care of the lab.
And when day I pushed the wrong button, and the whole thing went on fire, including me.
And I was caught.
She was caught, and I was caught because I was supposed to be sweeping the streets, and she was not supposed to take me into that position.
And I managed to roll on the ground and get the fire off me, but much of the lab was destroyed.
